prior_learning: Students will have covered some angle facts in years 7 and 8 so it is important to ascertain which ones they remember and build on these. Give students problems of increasing complexity where there is more than one way to find a solution and also multi-step problems. Interior angles of polygons with n sides and exterior angles have not been covered in years 7 and 8.
common_misconceptions: Students struggle to visualise transformations.  With rotations they should be encouraged to use tracing paper to help.

When describing transformations students need to name the type of transformation and then the features.  If it is a translation they need to say that and then describe the vector.  If it is a rotation, they need to say that and then describe the amount of turn, the direction and the centre.

learning_outcomes: Angles in a triangle and quadrilateral
key_knowledge_components: I can apply my knowledge of angles and shapes to more complex problems

key_vocab: Quadrilateral, square, rectangle, kite, rhombus, parallelogram, triangle, vertex, vertices, interior angle, exterior angle, equilateral, isosceles, scalene

key_questions: What do I call the angles on the inside of the triangle/quadrilateral?
What do the angles of a triangle add up to?
What do the angles of any quadrilateral add up to?
Which angle rules can I use to help me find this missing angles? Are there any other ways I can find the missing angle?
What type of triangle is this?  How do I know?
What notation do we use for equal sides? Equal angles?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Only start with the paper activity below if students have no prior knowledge of angles in a triangle. If they can remember angles in a triangle move straight to angles in a quadrilateral. Give each student a pre-cut triangle which are all different.  Get them to tear of the vertices and join the angles together so that they can see that whatever the triangle, its angles always add to 180o Repeat the above with different quadrilaterals so that th…

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Ensure students know the names of and understand the differences between types of triangles. They should also be familiar with the notation used for equal angles and sides.
notes.deepening_knowledge_summary: Use some questions with angles on a line and around a point to pre-teach the next lesson to help you guage the starting point for the class. key_questions: If the angles on a line make up a semi-circle so add up to 180o, what about angles around a point? How many degrees would you predict they add up to?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Students will have come across both angles in a line and around a point in years 7 and 8 so start by checking what they have remembered Check students can read and use shape notation for equal angles and sides.

key_knowledge_components: I can apply my knowledge of angles and shapes to more complex problems

key_vocab: Parallel, perpendicular, vertically opposite, corresponding, transversal

key_questions: How would you find out how big angle ‘a’ is?
So how big would angle ‘b’ be? How do you know? Is there another way we can work out angle b?
What are the arrows on these lines for? What do they mean?
How would we show these sides are equal in length?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Use angles on a line to teach vertically opposite angles as normally students can deduce vertically opposite from this. Spend time allowing students to recognize corresponding angles amongst a few angles

key_knowledge_components: I can apply my knowledge of angles and shapes to more complex problems

key_vocab: Parallel, perpendicular, vertically opposite, corresponding, alternate, co-interior, transversal

key_questions: Where are the corresponding angles?
These 2 angles are alternate – what do you think the rule is for them?  How do you know?
What shape to alternate angles make?
What shape do co-interior angles make?
Where is the C-shape in this question?
Where is the z shape in this question?
Give questions that incorporate corresponding, alternate and co-interior angles eg
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start with a re-cap on corresponding angles and link this into alternate and co-interior angles.  Some students will be able to predict the rules so use this in the lesson. Students need to be able to visualise the Z shape so expose them to it in different orientations Students need to be able to visualise co-interior angles

learning_outcomes: Interior angles of a polygon with n sides
key_knowledge_components: I know how to find the sum of interior angles for shapes of n sides.

key_vocab: Polygon, regular, irregular, quadrilateral, pentagon, hexagon, heptagon, octagon, nonagon, decagon, endecagon, dodecagon

key_questions: What is a polygon?
What do we mean by a regular polygon?
What do we mean by irregular polygon?
How many triangles can we split the pentagon into?
Each triangle’s angles add to 180o so if there’s 3 triangles what will the interior angles of the pentagon add to?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start by clarifying the differences between regular/irregular polygons Introduce sum of interior angles for any polygon by splitting polygons into triangles and using angles of a triangle add to 180. Get students to identify angle sum for polygons up to n sides by splitting into triangles and then adding the triangles up. Then get them to generalize between the number of sides of a polygon and how many triangles there will be eg n-2 so the angle…

key_knowledge_components: I know that exterior angles of any polygon add to 360.
I can find interior and exterior angles in polygons, both regular and irregular.

key_vocab: Interior angle, exterior angle, regular, irregular, polygon

key_questions: Where is the exterior angle?  What do we mean by an exterior angle?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Clarify what we mean by an exterior angle as it is the angle formed by a side of a polygon being extended and the next side of the polygon eg Link the lesson into interior angles to help students calculate the exterior angles and from that deduce that exterior angles of any polygon add up to 360o. learning_outcomes: Rotating a shape by 90, 180, 270 degrees around different points of rotation using tracing paper; describing the degree of rotation
Translating a shape by a given vector
key_knowledge_components: I can recognise rotational symmetry and a translation.
I can describe and implement different transformations.
I know what a vector is.
I know what a translation is.
I can describe a translation using a vector.
I can translate a shape by a given vector. Given a transformation I can decide whether it is a reflection, rotation or a translation and provide reasons.

key_vocab: Transformation, reflection, rotation, centre of rotation, clockwise, anticlockwise, object, image
Transformation, reflection, rotation, translation, translate, vector, magnitude

teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Get students to use tracing paper, especially when they cannot visualise the image; start with simple rotations and then give them of increasing difficulty. Then move students to descibing rotations: Week 4/ Lesson 3 and Week 5/Lesson 1 Start by describing the translations in words before introducing vectors.
